narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students—things like their early and later school grades, undergraduate performance, LSAT scores, family background, and study plans—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 56.3% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, meaning you placed in the bottom group compared to the typical 5th or 6th decile— this stood out as the strongest signal of potential struggle ahead. Your LSAT score of 35.5, a bit below the average of 36.7, also nudged the prediction toward failure, though not as powerfully. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.4 exceeded the typical 3.21, which helped pull the odds in favor of passing, as did your lower-middle family income bracket compared to higher averages. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd had a smaller but still negative influence toward failure, while planning to study full-time aligned with the vast majority of successful students and offered a slight boost. Overall, your profile stacks up competitively in areas like GPA and full-time commitment, but the lower later-year school performance dominated the model's view, setting you apart from students whose grades trended stronger over time. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted growth. Next, I recommend focusing on boosting your academic deciles by seeking tutoring or study groups, retaking the LSAT to aim above the average, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience as a Black woman in law. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the prediction dramatically in your favor. You've got this—keep pushing forward.
